paintings.title: Jahangir Preferring a Sufi Shaikh to Kings
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour, gold and ink on paper
paintings.location: Freer Gallery of Art, Washington, D.C.
paintings.story: The emperor Jahangir, haloed by sun and crescent moon, sits on a throne shaped like a European hourglass and hands a book to the Sufi saint Shaikh Husain of the Chishti sect — pointedly ignoring the Ottoman sultan, King James I of England, and the painter Bichitr himself, who all wait below in strict hierarchical order. The inscriptions convey that the emperor prefers gathering with holy men to visits from a court of kings. On the hourglass a Persian inscription reads, 'God is great. O Shah, may the span of your reign be a thousand years', suggesting the painting may have been presented to the emperor on his birthday.
funFacts: The portrait of James I is copied from a work by John de Critz brought to India by the English ambassador Thomas Roe, and the Ottoman sultan derives from a painting by Giovanni Bellini.
Bichitr painted himself in the bottom-left corner holding a picture — a self-insertion that later became a custom in Mughal painting.
European putti gather sand at the base of the hourglass throne, yet European perspective is deliberately rejected in the flat carpet below.

paintings.title: Shah Jahan Receives His Three Eldest Sons and Asaf Khan (Padshahnama)
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: Bichitr was one of the artists of the famous Windsor Padshahnama, the illustrated chronicle of Shah Jahan's reign, and this plate shows the newly enthroned emperor receiving his three eldest sons and his father-in-law Asaf Khan during the accession ceremonies. A large number of formal portraits of the 1630s are credited to Bichitr, whose likenesses of nearly every important personage of the period led Stuart Cary Welch to call him 'the Mughal Van Dyke'.
funFacts: Milo C. Beach says Bichitr paints with 'a brilliant, but hard line', his bold colours producing an effect of 'cold formality'.
Wheeler Thackston calls Bichitr the most observant of the Mughal artists.

paintings.title: Jujhar Singh Bundela Kneels in Submission to Shah Jahan
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The rebellious Bundela raja kneels before Shah Jahan in a durbar scene of icy protocol, one of Bichitr's most celebrated formal compositions of the 1630s. The painting so impressed later artists that it served as the inspiration for a sketch by Rembrandt, who copied Mughal miniatures in the 1650s. Bichitr's mastery of trompe-l'oeil reflections and cast shadows shows the European influence that runs through his work.
funFacts: Rembrandt based a drawing on this composition — one of the most direct meetings of Dutch and Mughal art.
Milo C. Beach concludes from Bichitr's clothing in his self-portraits that the painter was Hindu.

paintings.title: Portrait of Asaf Khan
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: Asaf Khan — Abu'l Hasan by name, brother of the empress Nur Jahan and father-in-law of Shah Jahan — was among the most powerful men of the empire, and his likeness belongs to the gallery of grandees Bichitr recorded. Painting the likeness of nearly every important personage of the period, Bichitr earned Stuart Cary Welch's title 'the Mughal Van Dyke'; his formal portraits define how we picture the Shah Jahan court today.
funFacts: In Bichitr's Shah Jahan with Asaf Khan, God the Father appears in the clouds with divine light reaching the emperor's halo — imagery that equates the emperor with Jesus.
Bichitr may have been an apprentice of Abu'l Hasan the painter, and his style is considered a variant of Hasan's.

paintings.title: Shah Shuja Enthroned with Maharaja Gaj Singh of Marwar
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
paintings.story: The young prince Shah Shuja, second son of Shah Jahan, shares a terrace throne with the Rajput Maharaja Gaj Singh of Marwar in one of the notable works of Bichitr's maturity. The pairing of Mughal prince and Hindu king captures the courtly alliances on which the empire rested, rendered with the bold, assertive colours and patterns that critics note in Bichitr's art.
funFacts: The final works attributed with certainty to Bichitr are from the 1640s — the Darbar of Aurangzeb and the unfinished Episode in a Bazaar.
Bichitr was patronised by two successive emperors, Jahangir and Shah Jahan.

paintings.title: Portrait of Raja Bikramajit (Sundar Das)
paintings.medium: ink, opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York (Shah Jahan Album)
paintings.story: This portrait of Raja Bikramajit, the courtier born Sundar Das, comes from the Shah Jahan Album in the Metropolitan Museum of Art. Bichitr's earliest works are datable to the late 1610s and early 1620s, already showing the poise and precision of a born portraitist. Most of his paintings are formal portraits, records of the men who ran the Mughal empire at its height.
funFacts: The earliest known painting by Bichitr, from c. 1615, is already a fully mature work.
Album folios like this one paired a painting on one side with calligraphy on the other.

paintings.title: Portrait of the Elephant 'Alam Guman
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.location: Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York
paintings.story: Though known above all for his formal portraits of people, Bichitr also painted animal figures, and this portrait of the imperial elephant 'Alam Guman treats the great beast with the same dignity he gave to princes. Prized elephants held named ranks at the Mughal court, and their portraits were collected in the imperial albums beside those of courtiers.
funFacts: Bichitr also painted Dara Shikoh riding a pink elephant.
He was active until at least the 1640s, serving the court for some three decades.

paintings.title: Dara Shukoh with Philosophers
paintings.medium: opaque watercolour and gold on paper
paintings.story: The crown prince Dara Shukoh, Shah Jahan's eldest and most intellectually adventurous son, sits in conversation with philosophers and holy men. The subject suited both sitter and painter: Dara Shukoh was famous for his engagement with mysticism, and Bichitr had made his name with Jahangir Preferring a Sufi Shaikh to Kings, an image of an emperor who valued dervishes above monarchs.
funFacts: Bichitr's work draws on European painting in its mastery of trompe-l'oeil reflections and cast shadows.
His self-portraits show him in a Hindu-styled robe, the evidence for scholars' conclusion that he was Hindu.
